episodes.title: The $1 Toy That Secretly Funded a Cult
episodes.description:
How did a simple piece of naval hardware become one of America’s most iconic toys — and how did its inventor lose everything to a religious cult?

This is the dark, tragic, and ultimately triumphant story behind the invention of the Slinky.

In 1943, mechanical engineer Richard James was working at a Philadelphia shipyard when he accidentally knocked a high-tension Swedish steel spring off a shelf. Instead of crashing to the floor, it “walked” in a smooth, mesmerizing motion — as if gravity itself had rhythm. In that moment, a piece of naval hardware became a phenomenon.

What followed was explosive success. The Slinky captured the imagination of post-war America, generating staggering profits from a toy that cost just a dollar. But sudden wealth brought crushing psychological pressure. Overwhelmed by guilt and spiritual obsession, Richard James secretly drained company funds to support an extremist, isolationist religious sect in Bolivia — abandoning his business, his employees, and his family.

He left behind millions in debt, unpaid federal taxes, and a collapsing factory.

That’s when Betty James stepped in.

Refusing to surrender, Betty transformed herself into an iron-willed CEO. She moved the heavy machinery to Hollidaysburg, Pennsylvania, saved the company from foreclosure, and commissioned the unforgettable Slinky television jingle that reignited national demand almost overnight.

From the perfect acoustic rhythm of the original blue-black high-carbon steel to later compromises with galvanized metal and plastic, this documentary reveals the real cost of a toy that once funded a cult.

This isn’t just the story of a novelty spring.

It’s a masterclass in resilience.

The man who bent the steel snapped under the tension.
The woman who stayed behind proved she was forged from something stronger.

Disclaimer
